content:
# Philippians Chapter 2 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 195-196 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHP_002_page_001.jpg, PHP_002_page_002.jpg

---

1. If therefore there is any encouragement in Christ, if any consolation of love, if any fellowship of the Spirit, if any affection and compassion,

2. fulfill my joy, that you be of the same mind, having the same love, united in soul, thinking the one thing.

3. Do nothing according to selfish ambition or according to vainglory, but in humility of mind let each regard the others as surpassing themselves,

4. each of you looking not to your own interests, but also each of you to the interests of others.

5. Have this mind among yourselves which was also in Christ Jesus,

6. who, being in the form of God, did not consider equality with God something to be grasped,

7. but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being made in the likeness of men.

8. And being found in appearance as a man, he humbled himself, becoming obedient unto death — even death on a cross.

9. Therefore God also highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name,

10. that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, of those in heaven and those on earth and those under the earth,

11. and every tongue should confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father.

12. Therefore, my beloved, just as you have always obeyed, not as in my presence only but now much more in my absence, work out your own salvation with fear and trembling,

13. for it is God who is at work in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure.

14. Do all things without grumbling and without disputing,

15. that you may become blameless and innocent, children of God without blemish in the midst of a crooked and perverted generation, among whom you shine as lights in the world,

16. holding fast the word of life, so that in the day of Christ I may have cause to boast that I did not run in vain nor labor in vain.

17. But even if I am being poured out as a drink offering upon the sacrifice and service of your faith, I rejoice and share my joy with you all.

18. And in the same way you also rejoice and share your joy with me.

19. But I hope in the Lord Jesus to send Timothy to you soon, so that I also may be encouraged when I know of your circumstances.

20. For I have no one of like mind who will genuinely be anxious for your circumstances.

21. For they all seek their own interests, not those of Christ Jesus.

22. But you know his proven character, that as a child with a father he served with me in the gospel.

23. Him therefore I hope to send at once, as soon as I see how things go with me.

24. But I am confident in the Lord that I myself also shall come soon.

25. But I considered it necessary to send to you Epaphroditus, my brother and fellow worker and fellow soldier, and your messenger and minister to my need,

26. since he was longing for you all and was distressed because you had heard that he was ill.

27. For indeed he was ill, near to death; but God had mercy on him, and not on him only but also on me, so that I might not have sorrow upon sorrow.

28. Therefore I have sent him all the more eagerly, so that when you see him again you may rejoice and I may be less anxious.

29. Receive him therefore in the Lord with all joy, and hold such men in honor,

30. because for the work of Christ he came near to death, having risked his life, in order to complete what was lacking in your service to me.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **The Christ Hymn (vv. 6-11):** This is among the most theologically dense passages in the entire New Testament. The Greek of Sinaiticus preserves the elevated, hymnic structure visible in the parallel clauses. I have retained the formal cadence of the original: "being in the form of God" (ἐν μορφῇ θεοῦ ὑπάρχων), "taking the form of a servant" (μορφὴν δούλου λαβών). The word μορφή ("form") denotes the essential nature or mode of being, not mere outward appearance — a crucial Christological distinction.

2. **v. 6 — "Something to be grasped" (ἁρπαγμόν):** This word has generated centuries of scholarly debate. It can mean either (a) something seized and held onto (res rapta — "he did not cling to equality"), or (b) something to be snatched at (res rapienda — "he did not consider it something to seize"). Sinaiticus reads ἁρπαγμόν without variant. I have rendered "something to be grasped," which preserves the ambiguity of the Greek while leaning toward the res rapienda reading: Christ possessed equality with God but did not exploit it.

3. **v. 7 — "Emptied himself" (ἑαυτὸν ἐκένωσεν):** The verb κενόω is the source of the theological term "kenosis." The emptying is defined by the participial phrases that follow: "taking the form of a servant, being made in the likeness of men." The emptying was not a subtraction of deity but an addition of servanthood. The formal translation preserves this grammatical relationship.

4. **v. 8 — "Even death on a cross":** The phrase θανάτου δὲ σταυροῦ is often understood as Paul's own addition to an earlier hymn, specifying the most shameful form of Roman execution. The dash in English captures the dramatic intensification of the Greek δέ here.

5. **v. 9 — "Highly exalted" (ὑπερύψωσεν):** The compound verb with ὑπερ- prefix intensifies beyond ordinary exaltation. This is not merely "exalted" but "super-exalted" — God has raised Christ to the supreme height. The "name above every name" is best understood as κύριος (Lord), declared in v. 11.

6. **v. 10 — "Of those in heaven and those on earth and those under the earth":** The tripartite cosmic division — ἐπουρανίων, ἐπιγείων, καταχθονίων — encompasses the totality of created reality. Every realm acknowledges Christ's lordship.

7. **v. 12 — "Work out your own salvation":** κατεργάζεσθε carries the sense of "bring to completion" or "accomplish thoroughly," not "earn." Paul has just described God's cosmic act of salvation; now the community is called to live it out. The very next verse (v. 13) grounds this imperative in divine initiative: "it is God who is at work in you."

8. **v. 15 — "Lights in the world" (φωστῆρες ἐν κόσμῳ):** The word φωστήρ refers to luminaries — stars or heavenly bodies — not simply "lights." The image is cosmic: believers shine like stars in the darkness of a morally disordered generation. I have rendered "lights" for readability while noting the astronomical resonance.

9. **v. 17 — "Poured out as a drink offering" (σπένδομαι):** Paul uses sacrificial language. The verb σπένδω refers to the pouring of a libation over a sacrifice. Paul sees his potential death as a drink offering poured upon the Philippians' own offering of faith. The present tense suggests an ongoing willingness rather than a completed event.

10. **Sinaiticus readings:** The text of Sinaiticus in this chapter is largely consistent with the critical text. Notable features include the continuous scriptio continua (no word division), which I have parsed according to standard scholarly analysis of the uncial hand. The column breaks on folio pages 195-196 divide the chapter across the third and fourth columns of page 1 and the first column of page 2.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
